title: Cosmic Radiation: Understanding High-Energy Particle Fluxes

lesson.learn: On July 28, 1912, Victor Hess measured a cosmic ray flux of approximately 6.54 particles/cm^2/min during a balloon flight over Austria. This detection marked the beginning of a century-long investigation into the origins and implications of cosmic radiation.
lesson.simpler: Cosmic radiation is formed by the acceleration of charged particles in the galaxy, primarily protons and electrons. These particles interact with the interstellar medium and are carried by the solar wind to the Earth.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The cosmic ray flux is modulated by solar wind variations, which influence the Earth's atmospheric composition. Recent studies with the Voyager 1 spacecraft have revealed intensity patterns up to 25 times greater than those observed on Earth.
lesson.deeperDive: The Cosmic Background Explorer (COBE) satellite mapped the cosmic microwave background radiation, detecting temperature fluctuations at a level of ±2.3μK. These fluctuations are thought to be evidence of the Big Bang. The Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope has mapped the gamma-ray distribution across the globe, revealing nearby sources and patterns influenced by galactic magnetic fields.
lesson.funFact: Cosmic radiation is not unique to our solar system; it is present in all galaxies throughout the observable universe. The High-Energy Particle Detector (HEPD) on the International Space Station measures cosmic particle fluxes at altitudes above 400 km.

people: Dr. Lisa Randall - Theoretical physicist investigating the impact of cosmic radiation on particle physics
Dr. Neil deGrasse Tyson - Astrophysicist promoting public awareness of space phenomena and the consequences of cosmic radiation
Professor Brian Greene - Theoretical physicist studying the connection between black holes and cosmic radiation patterns
Dr. Natalie Batalha - Exoplanetary scientist analyzing the impact of cosmic radiation on planetary atmospheres and the search for extraterrestrial life
places: CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research) - Leading research facility for particle physics and cosmic radiation studies
NASA's Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope - Satellites monitoring gamma-ray emissions and tracing cosmic radiation sources
The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) - Particle accelerator recreating high-energy collisions to study cosmic radiation properties
The Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) - Observing the vicinity of black holes, potentially associated with cosmic radiation emission
years: 1912: Victor Hess measured the first cosmic ray flux during a balloon flight over Austria
1942: Victor Hess and Robert B. Brown detected cosmic rays in a series of flight experiments
1960s-1970s: NASA launched a series of spacecraft to measure cosmic radiation in space
2008: The Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope was launched, greatly expanding our understanding of cosmic radiation sources
concepts: Cosmic ray flux - Measuring the intensity of incoming particles from space
Galactic magnetic fields - Influencing particle trajectories and cosmic radiation patterns
Supernovae explosions - Potentially producing the high-energy radiation observed on Earth
Interstellar medium - A diffuse gas that can absorb or deflect cosmic radiation particles
Particle acceleration - The process by which charged particles gain energy, contributing to cosmic radiation
